title: Slave-rotor theory of correlated altermagnets on the Lieb lattice
authors: Vanuildo S. de Carvalho, Hermann Freire, Rodrigo G. Pereira
abstract: We investigate the metal-insulator transition driven by the onsite repulsive interaction $U$ in an altermagnetic Hubbard model defined on a Lieb lattice. Using the slave-rotor approach at half filling, we find that the system exhibits a cascade of interaction-driven phase transitions. As $U$ increases, the system evolves from a normal metal to an altermagnetic metal, then to an altermagnetic insulator, and eventually to an altermagnetic Mott insulator characterized by the complete suppression of the quasiparticle weight. These phases are supported by the calculation of the electronic spectral function, which features spin-split bands in both the metallic and insulating regimes. However, the spin splitting becomes substantially suppressed in the Mott insulating phase. Our results suggest that the observation of spin splitting in the spectral function of $d$-wave altermagnets with a Lieb-lattice-like structure may be limited to the weak-to-moderate correlation regime.
summary: This theoretical work analyzes the metal-insulator transition in an altermagnetic Hubbard model on a Lieb lattice using the slave-rotor approach. It predicts a sequence of four phases as the onsite repulsion U increases, characterized by changes in the quasiparticle weight and the opening of a Mott gap. The findings suggest that observable spin splitting in the electronic spectrum is limited to the weak-to-moderate correlation regime.
main_problem: The paper investigates the metal-insulator transition driven by onsite repulsion in an altermagnetic Hubbard model on a Lieb lattice.
main_result: The system undergoes a sequence of phase transitions (NM $	o$ A$\ell$M $	o$ A$\ell$I $	o$ A$\ell$MI) as interaction $U$ increases, with the Mott insulator phase showing suppressed spin splitting.
method: The analysis employs the slave-rotor approach, mapping the Hubbard model onto an effective Lagrangian and solving self-consistent equations for order parameters.
model_or_system: The system is an altermagnetic Hubbard model defined on a Lieb lattice, studied at half-filling. The Hamiltonian includes nearest-neighbor and next-nearest-neighbor hopping terms, along with the onsite repulsive interaction U.
key_observables: Electronic spectral function (showing spin-split bands), quasiparticle weight (Z), staggered magnetization (M), and the Mott gap ($\Delta_X$).
important_parameters: The onsite repulsive interaction U, which drives the phase transitions; the hopping parameters $t_1, t_{2a}, t_{2b}$.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the slave-rotor theory and the saddle-point approximation for solving the resulting path integral/action.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the phase diagram showing the four distinct phases as U increases, and the spectral function $A_k(\omega)$ confirms spin splitting in metallic/insulating regimes but its suppression in the Mott phase.
paper_structure: The paper systematically derives the self-consistent equations for order parameters ($Q$ and $M$) by mapping the Hubbard model onto a slave-rotor formulation, calculating the spectral function, and mapping out the phase diagram based on the behavior of $Z$ and $\Delta_X$.
why_interesting: The detailed theoretical mapping of a complex correlated system (Hubbard model) onto a solvable bosonic/fermionic framework (slave-rotor) provides a powerful tool for understanding exotic magnetic and electronic states in strongly correlated materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic representation of the Lieb-lattice struc- ture in quasi-2D altermagnets with a T2O structure, where the magnetic T (nonmagnetic O) atoms occupy the A and B (C) sublattice sites. The altermagnetic configuration is indi- cated by blue and red arrows, representing opposite magnetic moments. The electrons described by the model Hamiltonian (1) move between the A and B sublattices with hopping am- plitudes t1 and t2a/2b for NN and NNN sites, respectively.

FIG. 3. Spinon dispersion along a path with high-symmetry points of the BZ [see inset in panel (a)] for interaction U corresponding to the (a) AℓM, (b) AℓI, and (c) AℓMI phases. Here we set t′ = 0.3t1, δ = 0.2, and T = 0.01t1. The red and blue curves represent the dispersions for spin-up and spin- down spinon bands, respectively. The AℓM exhibits spinon Fermi surfaces, which are gapped out in the insulating phases, as a result of the opening of either a (b) band or (c) Mott gap. All spinon dispersions shown here exhibit a dx2−y2-wave spin- splitting characteristic of altermagnetism.

FIG. 2. Order parameters and phase diagram of the alter- magnetic Hubbard model at half filling. In the NNN hop- pings t2a/2b = t′(1 ± δ), we fix t′ = 0.3t1 and consider two values of the anisotropy parameter: (a)-(b) δ = 0.2; (c)-(d) δ = 0.8. The temperature is kept at T = 0.01t1. The al- termagnetic order sets in when the staggered magnetization M becomes finite. Depending on the anisotropy, this transi- tion can be either continuous or discontinuous. As the onsite repulsion U increases, the order parameters exhibit a series of nonanalyticities which signal transitions between a normal metal (NM), an altermagnetic metal (AℓM), an altermagnetic insulator (AℓI), and an altermagnetic Mott...

FIG. 4. Electron spectral function Ak(ω) for t′ = 0.3t1 and δ = 0.2. (a) Spectral function Ak(ω = 0) in the AℓM phase; the peaks can be identified with the electron Fermi surface. The red and blue dashed lines represent, respectively, the spin-up and spin-down spinon Fermi surfaces. (b) The agree- ment between spinon and electron spin splittings in the AℓM phase is also exemplified by the dependence of Ak(ω) along the BZ cut ky = π; the dashed lines represent the spinon dispersions. We also show the energy dependence of the spec- tral function for k = (0, π) and values of U in three different phases: (c) AℓM; (d) AℓI; and (e) AℓMI. Note that the spin splitting decreases as U increases. (f)...
